comments:
This paper is a clear, well-written description of the Allen AI Science Challenge: its motivation, structure, results, and the authors' interpretation of what the competition reveals about the current state of AI. Strengths: the paper motivates why standardized tests are a useful, measurable alternative to the Turing test, describes the dataset scale (2,283 questions) and prize structure, and gives an informative breakdown of the top three entries and the features and methods that led to their results. The analysis that IR-based methods dominated and that true progress will require deeper semantic understanding and multi-step reasoning is well argued and consistent with the competition outcomes. The paper also situates the challenge relative to prior QA work (TREC, Freebase, Wolfram Alpha, etc.) and outlines future directions (Aristo, a larger challenge).

Weaknesses and suggestions: the paper is primarily descriptive and lacks deeper technical or empirical analysis. There is little systematic error analysis or formal taxonomy of question types (fact lookup vs. reasoning vs. commonsense) that would help quantify where current methods fail and where progress is most needed. The discussion of corpora and feature sets would benefit from more quantitative detail (e.g., ablation studies, feature importances, baseline comparisons) although the paper does summarize winners' approaches. A few repeated/duplicated text segments appear in the provided copy, suggesting editorial cleanup is needed. The authors could strengthen the manuscript by (1) providing a breakdown of performance by question category or difficulty, (2) reporting baseline performance (e.g., random, simple IR baselines) and statistical significance of top teams' differences, (3) including more concrete examples of failure cases with analysis of the inference steps missing, and (4) adding reproducibility information (data access, evaluation scripts, winner code if releasable).

Overall recommendation: this is a useful community-facing report that documents an important benchmark and provides actionable insight about the limits of current QA systems. It will be of interest to researchers working on question answering, commonsense reasoning, and evaluation design. With minor editorial and analytical improvements it would be even more valuable.
